# Agentic Tooling: User Stories (Human User's Perspective)

64 user stories written from the human user's-eye view of working with an agentic coding runtime. Fourth in the set alongside [agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md) (the calling agent), [agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md) (the tool), and [agent-tooling-user-stories-runtime-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-runtime-pov.md) (the orchestrating runtime).

## Trust & Transparency

1. As a user, I want to see what a tool call will do before it executes, so I can decide whether to allow it rather than discover the effect after the fact.
2. As a user, I want the agent to distinguish what it verified from what it's assuming, so I don't mistake a guess for a checked fact.
3. As a user, I want a clear account of what changed after a task, so I can review the diff instead of trusting a prose summary.
4. As a user, I want the agent to flag when it's uncertain, rather than presenting a guess with the same confidence as a verified fact.
5. As a user, I want to know which parts of a response came from my own codebase versus the agent's general training, so I can weight my trust accordingly.
6. As a user, I want visibility into why an action was blocked (which rule, which policy), so I can adjust configuration instead of just being told no.
7. As a user, I want the agent to tell me when it's about to do something outside the scope I actually asked for, so scope creep doesn't happen silently.
8. As a user, I want honest pushback when my request is a bad idea, not silent compliance that saves the disagreement for after something breaks.

## Control & Intervention

9. As a user, I want to interrupt a running task at any point without losing the useful work already done.
10. As a user, I want to approve risky or irreversible actions individually, not have one early "yes" treated as blanket future consent.
11. As a user, I want a way to redirect a task mid-stream when I see it heading the wrong way, without having to restart from scratch.
12. As a user, I want fine-grained permission controls (per tool, per action class), so I'm not stuck choosing between "allow everything" and "approve every single step."
13. As a user, I want to pause a long-running or background task and resume it later without losing state.
14. As a user, I want an easy way to say "stop, don't do that" and have it actually stop, not finish the current step regardless.
15. As a user, I want to set durable preferences once (in a config file, a memory) rather than repeat the same correction every session.
16. As a user, I want to delegate a task to run autonomously in the background when I trust it, without being forced to babysit every step.

## Communication & Clarity

17. As a user, I want responses sized to the question — a quick fix gets a quick answer, not a report.
18. As a user, I want technical explanations pitched to my actual level of context, not re-explained from scratch every time.
19. As a user, I want a plan I can react to before implementation starts on anything nontrivial, so I can redirect before work is sunk.
20. As a user, I want the agent to ask when genuinely blocked on a decision only I can make, rather than guessing and hoping.
21. As a user, I want status updates during long tasks at meaningful checkpoints — not silence, and not a running transcript of every internal thought.
22. As a user, I want the agent to tell me directly when it can't verify something (e.g. no way to run the UI), rather than implying success it didn't actually confirm.
23. As a user, I want jargon-free summaries by default, with the option to go deeper if I ask.
24. As a user, I want my own prior corrections respected without re-explaining them, so I'm not stuck repeating myself across sessions.

## Safety & Reversibility

25. As a user, I want destructive operations (force-push, hard reset, delete) to require explicit confirmation every time, not just the first time.
26. As a user, I want an easy way to see and undo the last change set if it turns out wrong.
27. As a user, I want in-progress or uncommitted work protected by default, so an agent's cleanup pass can't quietly discard something I hadn't saved yet.
28. As a user, I want secrets and credentials never surfaced in logs or chat output, even incidentally during debugging.
29. As a user, I want the agent to investigate before deleting or overwriting unfamiliar state, in case it's my in-progress work rather than debris.
30. As a user, I want shared/production systems treated with more caution than my local sandbox by default.
31. As a user, I want a reversible action (rename, move) preferred over a destructive one (delete) whenever both would achieve the goal.
32. As a user, I want risky actions scoped exactly to what I approved, not silently generalized to a wider blast radius.

## Cost & Resource Awareness

33. As a user, I want to know roughly what an expensive multi-agent operation will cost before I commit to running it.
34. As a user, I want to set a budget ceiling for a task and have it actually respected, not treated as advisory.
35. As a user, I want the agent to default to the cheapest approach that solves my problem, reserving heavy multi-agent fan-out for when I've actually asked for that scale.
36. As a user, I want transparency about which model or effort level is doing the work, so I can judge whether it matches the task's difficulty.
37. As a user, I want background/scheduled tasks to have sensible default intervals, so I'm not paying for pointless tight-loop polling I never asked for.
38. As a user, I want a running tally of spend available on request, not something I have to reconstruct myself from logs.
39. As a user, I want to opt into expensive operations explicitly (e.g. a cloud review), not have them triggered as a side effect of an unrelated request.
40. As a user, I want the option to scale a task down when the situation doesn't warrant the full heavy-duty treatment, even if a bigger version is available.

## Continuity & Memory

41. As a user, I want the agent to remember durable facts about my project across sessions, so I'm not re-explaining the same context every time.
42. As a user, I want to review and correct what's been remembered about me or the project, in case something was captured wrong.
43. As a user, I want stale memories checked against current reality before being acted on, not treated as permanently true.
44. As a user, I want to explicitly tell the agent to forget something, and have that actually take effect.
45. As a user, I want context from a long conversation preserved through compaction, so picking a task back up doesn't feel like starting over.
46. As a user, I want work I paused to be resumable without re-deriving everything that was already decided.
47. As a user, I want memory to capture the why behind a decision, not just the what, so future sessions understand the reasoning, not just the outcome.
48. As a user, I want personal preferences and project facts kept separate, so a memory about me doesn't leak into a memory about the codebase or vice versa.

## Collaboration & Workflow Fit

49. As a user, I want the agent to follow my existing codebase conventions rather than impose its own stylistic defaults.
50. As a user, I want the agent to match my commit message style and workflow habits (branch naming, PR structure) rather than a generic default.
51. As a user, I want scope held to what I actually asked for — no unsolicited refactors or drive-by cleanups riding along with a bug fix.
52. As a user, I want the agent to use my project's own tooling and scripts instead of reaching for generic equivalents that bypass local conventions.
53. As a user, I want to be able to say "use a workflow" or similar and have that opt-in genuinely control when heavier orchestration kicks in.
54. As a user, I want the agent to check for an existing project-specific script or skill before improvising a generic solution.
55. As a user, I want handoffs between agent and human to be clean — a clear point where "your turn" becomes "my turn," not an ambiguous trailing state.
56. As a user, I want the agent to flag when a request conflicts with a decision I made earlier in the same project, rather than silently overriding it.

## Customization & Configuration

57. As a user, I want to configure default behaviors (verbosity, model choice, permission mode) once, in a settings file, rather than restate them every session.
58. As a user, I want per-project overrides to take precedence over global defaults, so one repo's conventions don't leak into another's.
59. As a user, I want a documented, discoverable way to add my own skills/commands, so customization doesn't require reverse-engineering the system.
60. As a user, I want permission rules I set to be inspectable, so I can audit what I've actually authorized over time.
61. As a user, I want sensible defaults out of the box, so a new project isn't unusable until I've hand-tuned a dozen settings.
62. As a user, I want to scope a capability to a specific directory or app, so a broad tool doesn't behave identically everywhere regardless of context.
63. As a user, I want changes to my configuration to take effect without needing to fully restart or relearn the tool.
64. As a user, I want to turn off any automated or proactive behavior I don't want, without losing the rest of the system's usefulness.
